document.critique: Checked all five candidates against machine counts, pairwise rules, domain limits, implications, and both exact-one constraints. Candidate 1’s lower cost fails because J7=Y requires J5≠X; Candidate 3 fails for the same reason, and Candidate 4 has only two X jobs. Candidates 0 and 2 survive, with costs 153 and 155 respectively. Thus Candidate 0 remains the strongest valid unique optimum.

Rechecked every constraint and machine count: this assignment is feasible. Recomputed its cost term-by-term as 176, so Candidate 0’s claimed 166 fails arithmetic; Candidate 2 has the same assignment and correct cost. Candidates 1 and 3 are feasible but cost 188 and 180, respectively. Thus retain the assignment with exact total 176.

Recomputed the returned assignment’s costs: 3+4+35+4+22+18+18+10+14+13+7+14 = 162, so the listed 157 was wrong. All machines have three jobs, and every interaction—including both exact-one rules—survives. Candidate 4 is cheaper on paper but violates J10/J12 must differ. Thus retain its mapping as the strongest feasible candidate, with corrected exact cost 162.

I rechecked machine counts, every interaction, both exact-one rules, and recomputed costs. Candidates 1 and 2 are feasible; candidate 1 totals 171. Candidate 0 is feasible but its stated cost is wrong (186, not 184). Candidate 2 survives at 168 and is strictly cheaper, so it replaces the preceding proposal as the unique optimum.

I re-evaluated each candidate against the rule that each statement’s Boolean value equals its speaker’s type. Candidates 0, 1, 3, and 4 fail immediately on B; candidate 2 survives the broader checks but its F statement evaluates false, conflicting with F marked truthful. Thus no candidate is fully self-consistent; candidate 2 is retained only as the closest proposal, not a verified exact solution.

Re-evaluated every candidate against each speaker’s full Boolean statement. Candidates 0, 2, and 3 fail directly at C or A; candidate 4 fails because Q’s implication is true despite Q being labeled liar. Candidate 1 survives all 17 checks, including G’s XOR evaluating false, so it is the unique consistent assignment.

Rechecked every candidate against its own statement truth values. Candidates 0–3 each fail directly (D, G, or F respectively). Candidate 4 is consistent except J: with C=liar, O=M=truthful, J's statement is IFF(false,false)=true, so J must be truthful. Changing J preserves Q and N consistency; therefore this corrected assignment is exact.

I evaluated every nested statement under Candidate 1 and each statement’s Boolean value matches its speaker type. The nearest alternative, Candidate 0, fails decisively: E’s statement evaluates true despite E labeled liar, and M’s statement evaluates true despite M labeled liar. Thus Candidate 1 survives the full consistency check and should replace any preceding proposal.

I rechecked all recurrence constraints by enumerating the three possible moduli, coefficient choices, and r,s ranges, then forward-substituted the surviving system. Candidates 0 and 1 fail immediate continuation checks; candidate 2 survives every supplied term and uniquely predicts the next three remainders as [90,46,78].

Rechecked the recurrence by comparing terms four indices apart across all three candidate moduli. M=97 and M=101 produce no allowed integer r and coefficient choices; M=103 survives with p[0]=2, q[0]=-1, t=2, r=-1, and consistent remaining constraints. Extending that unique system gives [14,0,0], so Candidate 3 should be retained.

Neither candidate survives verification: substituting the observed terms into the recurrence requires a consistent modulus and bounded coefficients, which [0,0,0] and [-1,-1,-1] do not establish. The preceding review supplied no derivation or verified system, so it cannot support either proposal; this answer is retained only as the least-unsupported candidate.
